lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — Galactosylation and complement-dependent cytotoxicity
Welcome back.
For the first half we focused almost entirely on one sugar — core fucose — and one effector function — ADCC.
Now I want to widen the lens, because the Asn297 glycan has other sugar modifications that control other effector functions.
The most important of those is galactosylation of the terminal GlcNAcs, which controls a different killing pathway entirely: complement-dependent cytotoxicity, or CDC.
Quick refresher on complement, since it deserves a moment.
The complement system is a cascade of about thirty plasma proteins that, when activated, ultimately punch holes in the membranes of target cells, killing them by osmotic lysis.

The cascade has three activation arms — classical, alternative, lectin — and the one relevant to antibody therapeutics is the classical arm, which is triggered by antibody Fc regions binding to a complement protein called C1q.
C1q is a six-headed protein, shaped like a bouquet, that engages multiple Fc stems simultaneously when antibodies are clustered on a target cell surface.
Once C1q is engaged, it activates the rest of the cascade — C4, C2, C3, the terminal components — leading to formation of the membrane attack complex that puts holes in the target.
For complement-dependent cytotoxicity by an antibody drug, the rate-limiting step is C1q binding to the Fc.
And C1q binding to the Fc is modulated by the Asn297 glycan, with terminal galactose being a key determinant.
Here's the story.
Antibody glycoforms with terminal galactose — G1F, G2F, G1, G2 — bind C1q more avidly than antibody glycoforms lacking galactose — G0F, G0.

The mechanism involves subtle effects on the conformation and dynamics of the CH2 domain: more galactose, the domain adopts a more "open" conformation, the C1q binding surface is more accessible, and C1q docks more efficiently.

The effect is not as dramatic as the afucose effect on ADCC — we're talking maybe two- to five-fold enhancement, not fifty-fold — but it's reproducible and it matters clinically for drugs whose mechanism of action depends on complement.
Rituximab is a prime example.
Rituximab kills B cells through multiple mechanisms — ADCC, direct apoptosis, and CDC — and the CDC contribution is substantial.
The galactose content of a rituximab preparation correlates with its CDC potency.
Manufacturers care about this.
The galactose distribution is monitored as a quality attribute, and process changes that shift galactose levels are scrutinized for impact on potency.
The clinical analogy I want you to hold is this: terminal galactose on the glycan enhances complement-dependent cytotoxicity.
Design for the killing mechanism you want.
If you want NK-mediated killing, you afucosylate.
If you want complement-mediated killing, you galactosylate.
If you want both, you do both — and we'll come back to hybrid designs in Segment 12.
There's an interesting twist on the galactose story that's worth knowing about, because it ties Fc glycobiology to autoimmunity.
In rheumatoid arthritis, as Roy Jefferis observed decades ago, patients have elevated G0 — that is, glycoforms lacking galactose — in their serum IgG.
The G0 fraction can rise from a normal value of maybe 20% up to 50% or higher in active disease.

And this elevated G0 has been mechanistically linked to enhanced inflammation: G0 IgG immune complexes interact with mannose-binding lectin and with certain Fc receptors in ways that drive inflammatory cytokine release.
So at the level of serum IgG, your body modulates its own galactosylation in response to inflammation, and that modulation feeds back to amplify or dampen inflammation.
Galactose down, inflammation up.
Galactose up, inflammation down.
This is one of the cleanest examples of the Fc glycan as a regulatory feature — not just a passive decoration, but a dial that the immune system actively tunes during health and disease.
For therapeutic engineering, the lesson is: if you're making a drug whose mechanism depends on complement-mediated killing, pay attention to galactose.
If your manufacturing process drifts toward lower galactose, your drug may lose potency.
If you can intentionally drive galactose higher — through cell-line engineering, through media supplementation with manganese and galactose, through process control — you may gain potency.
This is a real lever, and it is increasingly part of how modern therapeutic antibodies are designed.
